speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture--

speaker: interviewer
text: April 24, 1996. The survivor is Sonja Waitzner. Her maiden name is Friedman. And this is tape number five.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of us taken in 1942, in Brussels. On the left is my brother, Norbert Friedman. I am in the middle. And on my right, on the other side, is Leo Friedman, my other brother. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I was-- This is my [FRENCH], my identification card under the German occupation, dated July 1943. And stamped in red is 'Juif-Jood,' French for Jew and Flemish. This is from the District of Brussels where I lived, declaring that I was deportated-- deported from Brussels to, to Auschwitz in 1944 and returned in June of 1945 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to Brussels. This was at a dinner party at my friends, Harry and Susie [INAUDIBLE]. To the left, is myself. In the back of me is Norbert, my brother. In front in the blouse is Blanca. In the back of her is Susie [INAUDIBLE], the hostess. In front is Gerte, my sister-in-law. Next to her is Morris Waitzner, my husband. My beloved father. And in the back, is Leo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year could that have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: It could have been in the '50s, in the '50s. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Is that OK? Of course, on the left is myself. And next to me is my beloved father. And I must have been about 24 years old here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- that is, in Central Park. We are having coffee. We were always going to coffee houses. [LAUGHS] Europeans. Those were happy times. I was young. This is my beloved and only child, Steven. He's-- of course, you can see it's his bar mitzvah. And that was in 1970. Happy days, those were happy, happy days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York. We had about 100 people celebrating with us. I was-- I'm happy I made such a wonderful bar mitzvah. This is my son, Steven, as he looks now. He's 39 years old. This was taken at my brother's house in Lido Beach, New York. That's how he looks now. I love him dearly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here is my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here's my brother, Leo Friedman, my sister-in-law, his wife, Gerte Friedman. Their daughter and my niece, Linda Friedman. And here is my niece, Renee Friedman, who is absent right now, and I feel very badly about it. I also feel badly that my son isn't here. But he is in my heart. I'm glad-- I'm almost 70 years old. And I hope to enjoy some peaceful time in whatever's left for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to say something about my sister. I don't know what she told you in her interview. But she is a remarkable woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Because all her life, she was not lucky. But she-- it's none of her doing. She just-- things didn't turn out the way they should. In all her life, she had misfortune and bad luck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Despite all this, she always kept a good spirit. She was always [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] gracious. She doesn't have a nasty-- any bad bone in her body. She's a good person. And she always kept a good spirit. She always smiles. She's always ready to laugh and to smile. And I find very remarkable. Despite all the bad things that happened to her in life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- you want me to say something, Mom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister and I love Tante Sonja. And she's always joking that-- she tells everyone that she's our favorite aunt. But then again, she's our only aunt. [LAUGHING] And we think of her the same way. We hold her as a woman that's like an example of how to be in your life and how to be positive. Because she's always positive, and she loves us, and she brings so much. And she's such a cultured person and never really has let her life get her down. And I, too, was thinking that I wish Steve were here. Because it means a lot to him to see his mom and to see his mom talk about this, you know. And I mentioned that Tante Sonja was doing this, Steve said that he was glad, and that he just wishes he knew more, so he could help her carry the burden. So from all of us, Tante, we love you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm almost 70 years old. And I feel fortunate that I have still members of my family left. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I hope for the best in the future. Thank you.
